column7:
I think one issue here is that you've equated "removing the rights of millions" with "banning guns". These aren't necessarily the same thing. You can deny millions of people their "right" while simultaneously not banning firearms at all.

The largest issues with regards to gun violence in the United States are suicide, gang violence/crime, and regular homicides. Most of these can be prevented by simply making the gun ownership process a little more regulated. Don't give people with mental issues firearms. Don't allow criminals to keep firearms once convicted. Ensure that a transaction must involve a background check to be legal. 

These solutions ultimately do "take away the rights of millions", but at the same time they will likely save thousands of lives per year.

@chocolate-barbel, I see your point about the low probability of being involved in a mass shooting, but I think that's a pretty narrow focus. The issue is broader than just mass shootings. Gun violence affects many more people through suicides, gang violence, and domestic homicides. Regulation can help prevent these tragedies without completely banning guns. What are your thoughts on stricter background checks and mental health screenings for gun owners?

"@chocolate-barbel and @happy-coriander, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @happy-coriander has raised some valid points about the broader issue of gun violence beyond mass shootings. @chocolate-barbel, would you like to respond to these points and share your thoughts on potential regulations such as stricter background checks and mental health screenings?"

column7: I'm not arguing for Trump in any stretch of the imagination. And I don't really have any arguments that support him. But if I were to back that up as his selling point, I'd say it's because he doesn't "have to" like someone who made their money in politics like Hillary. In fact, he barely has to advertise himself, because the stuff he says is so batshit it gets media attention. Combine that with his strong name recognition pre-election, and suddenly the big media networks are paying for his campaign for him. But yeah. Vote for Hillary before Trump. She's not my favorite candidate, but I'll take her over Trump any day. Just the fact he has so much momentum makes me embarrassed to be American

Sure they'll act within their own interests, but there comes a point where fucking up the economy to a certain extent doesn't benefit anyone. Trump's plan crosses that line and continues into space. I'm not a huge fan of Adam Smith, but I think the invisible hand will ultimately take the wheel and steer us away from a trainwreck on the scale of Trump's economic policy.

column7:
Your outlet will not be plugged.
Had those hobbies been forbidden, I am sure you would have discovered another coping mechanism.

I do not believe creative expression has any objective value, mental health can be benefited from a population who is achieving and making a difference.

I as an engineer, need an outlet at the end of my day to stay sane. Whether that is photography or playing music, I would not have gotten into those hobbies if I were not forced to take them in school. Teaching children art isnt about teaching them what they need for a profession, its about learning how to express yourself in a creative way. This makes a positive contribution to society by improving the general mental health of the population.

"@tart-nickel, are you kidding me? You think people would just magically find another coping mechanism if creative outlets were forbidden? Newsflash: people need art, music, and other creative expressions to stay sane. And @frayed-head is spot on, it's not about preparing kids for a profession, it's about helping them develop as human beings. What's wrong with a society that values mental health and self-expression?"

column7: No sex is a deal breaker.  Period.  End of story. Full stop.  If my significant other is unilaterally deciding to "close up shop", I'm going to get my comforts someplace else regardless of the "marriage vows".  The door swings both ways and has nothing to do with "TRP".

column7:
> It is narcissistic to break an agreement, then be mad that the other person decided to break it too. Sex with a husband/wife is part of a marriage.

If you read marriage vows, many specifically include not cheating: "forsaking all others, "faithful to you", etc. I have yet to read one that specifically reads "I will continue to fuck my partner throughout this marriage." You could argue that "to cherish" includes "to fuck," but at the same time "to cherish" clearly includes respecting the decision to not have sex so that's not the strongest argument. 

Back to the hypothetical - should the rich man not be angry he was stolen from because he knew it was a possibility and it was his fault he was stolen from (because he was rich)?

PS - you might be better off posting a CMV of your TRP tendencies, you'd get more targeted responses.

column7:
What does it mean to be racist?

Well, one clear example would be doing something that you know promotes racism. Saying the N word, voting for Trump, fighting for the Confederates. Racism may not be the primary motivator for doing those things (really stretching by saying that, but we will pretend for a second) but doing those things furthers racism, and so someone has to do them *in spite* of racism. However, to be ok with racism is in and of itself racist.

column7:
>In the title, I've said adults I.e. 18 or whatever the local age would be." Sex with a minor is an illegal activity, incest isn't the only source of this. I agree that consent is impossible between an underage daughter and her father, but how is it any different from strangers of the same age, just because she trusts her father and he provides for her and not the stranger is an invalid argument. A completely broke women whoring herself out to an old guy for his money, is a milder situation of the one proposed above in principle.

You write this as if there isn't already a stigma in massive age gaps between couples, and also as if the power dynamic within those relationships aren't similarly studied and denounced. While they aren't illegal, they are hardly free of scrutiny.

There is also a very important difference between stranger and family, and that is the issue of grooming. There is a great deal more potential for abuse for fathers conditioning their daughters to be their future wives than an adult woman making an adult choice.

Social scrutiny, does not even enter the discussion for me. There are people ready to shoot gay couples. Only because we outnumbered them doesn't make it legitimate from illegitimate. I don't think fear of being legally persecuted is the only thing stopping from fathers from molesting their daughters, I don't say I'm better than others because they need a law stopping them from raping their kid, there are psychopaths out there ramming trucks into people, If something could potentially lead to sick people doing something which is a legal offense, still doesn't justify banning it.

column7:
Being denied a platform is literally what censorship is, it's just another way of saying it. Maybe you're just avoiding it because you think censorship=bad but that doesn't really follow. Censorship can be used badly or well but 'denying a platform' is just another way of saying the same thing.

Political ideologies and ideas in general aren't fanclubs, even though people treat them that way. They are constructs with which people try to describe or shape reality, whether you 'like' them or not is actually a flaw in approaching them. The main concern should be whether they're useful or true, protection from that test is protecting the aspects of the ideology that don't gel with reality and courting disaster if it's ever used as a guide for behaviour, because reality always wins in the end.

The only legit exemption that I can see is if you have a stream of low-level newcomers constantly launching the same discredited attacks over and over again without ever having engaged with the views within the ideology properly. That can be dealt with with FAQs and things like that though. Banning all discussion is just sheltering from reality, especially with subs like latestagecapitalism were the ideas are so strawmanningly retarded that if they put down the banhammer for two seconds the whole thing would be exposed straight away. 'No dissent' rules in those cases are just shielding for a dumb shibboleth that people are unwilling to let go of for whatever reasons.
column19: ['Busy', 'Leans towards Democratic party', 'Enjoys listening to traditional Vietnamese music']

column7:
>The rule turns the sub into an echo chamber where no fruitful debate or discussion can occur. Even something along the lines of "I have a genuine question, how would insert political ideology solve X if Y is true?" is banned is some subs.

That begins with the presumption that being confrontational in what is, essentially, a meeting for a given ideology is going to be a good thing.

Even if we ignore that there is a mechanism for burying dissenting views anyway (i.e. that every "debate" would be downvoted to the point of functionally not appearing), do you really think it's reasonable to demand that people who go to a meeting in support of (say) socialism be ready and willing to argue with you at that meeting?

>Many users often respond that there are other subs for debating, which is true. However, what justifies censorship?

Because there isn't any actual censorship.  The idea that being denied a specific platform to speak is "censorship" is part of the toxic attitude of the alt-right.

>Simply because there exists a sub for debating does not mean the partisan sub should be free from debate.

Except it kind of does.  You're going into a fanclub and saying "the basic premise of this is questionable".  But you have somewhere else to make that point that *isn't* demanding that the fanclub give you room to speak your mind about why the thing they like sucks.

I don't get to barge into your house to tell you that you're a piece of shit, that's not censorship.

We can get into the question of whether the kinds of hostile internet "debates" are actually at all beneficial, but I'm not sure we even need to go that far.

@excited-coriander, @obsolete-conference, you're both kinda missing the point. If people can't handle a little debate in their "fanclub" subs, then maybe their ideology isn't as strong as they think it is. I mean, what's the harm in letting people ask questions or criticize? It's not like it's gonna destroy the whole community or something. And @obsolete-conference, just because someone has somewhere else to make their point doesn't mean they can't make it in the sub. That's like saying I can only draw in a specific notebook and nowhere else. Doesn't make sense.

"@brute-force-roundel, @excited-coriander, @obsolete-conference, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Remember to address the ideas, not each other. @brute-force-roundel, your point about the strength of an ideology is a valid one, but let's consider @obsolete-conference's perspective on the purpose of specific subs as well. How can we balance open discussion with the need for focused community spaces?"

column7: Okay, I think this makes sense.  You don't think it's possible that the way that these biases affect your decisions could have a negative impact on yourself?  Let's forget the effect on others entirely.

> I believe this view is both practical and unavoidable for interacting with the world in many ways.

OP, you actually haven't mentioned how you interact with the world.  You only mention your thoughts and judgments- not how they manifest themselves into action.

Any suitable answer to this CMV will be about your own personal experience, separated from how others are treated or society is affected, which will make it kinda difficult.

So I have to ask- is it possible that any of these judgements could negatively impact you?  For example- a southern person comes up to you in a bar.  You dismiss them because you don't think they could have an intellectual conversation.  You've limited your experience of the world based on a judgement.

If you wouldn't dismiss them based on the accent, then you're not a little bit prejudiced, because you've overridden your wired instincts with a logical response.  

Action based on critical thinking of one's misguided predispositions matters more than the predispositions themselves.

@average-action, that's a great point about considering how our biases affect our own experiences. I have to admit, I've definitely had situations where I've limited myself by making judgments about people based on superficial characteristics. Like, I once assumed a coworker wasn't very organized just because their desk was messy, and it turned out they were actually really on top of things and just had a different work style. If I had written them off completely, I would have missed out on some valuable collaborations. So yeah, I can see how these biases can have a negative impact on myself, even if I'm not intentionally trying to harm others.

"@human-bargain and @average-action, thank you both for sharing your thoughtful perspectives and personal experiences. This discussion is a great example of how considering multiple viewpoints can lead to a deeper understanding of the issue. Let's keep the conversation going and see where it takes us."

column7:
You're looking at this whole "privilege" and "advantage" thing all backwards. It's not an advantage or privilege to be treated like you should be treated as a person. That's just the norm. If a person is discriminated against then they're being treated below the norm. 

Why should we consider being treated normally as an advantage or a privilege? It's just...normal.

It is unreasonable to be irritated about problem A being addressed on the basis that problems B & C are not being addressed. You understand? "I am so mad that my car is in the shop right now! But I wouldn't be mad if my computer & phone were also in for repairs."

> People with hearing, seeing, and speech disabilities get discriminated against all the time yet you never hear talk of "sight privilege" or "speech privilege" or "hearing privilege." 

That's because the word is "ability privilege".

EDIT: To address a deleted comment:

"Privilege", in the context of identity politics, doesn't really mean "advantage compared to a baseline". It means that where people unlike you encounter obstacles, you don't. So for instance "white privilege" means that when a person with a white-sounding name applies for, say, an IT job, no-one bats an eye. But you stick DeShawn on your CV, and suddenly everyone is very interested in whether you have a criminal record.

It's not about what the "baseline" is. It's about whether society is set up to put barriers in your way every day.

@independent-delay, I see your point, but let's not forget that being treated "normally" is actually a privilege when many others are consistently marginalized and excluded from that same normal treatment. And @sticky-tabby, your analogy about the car, computer, and phone is a good one - just because one issue is being addressed, it doesn't mean we should ignore the others. But I do think we need to be careful about how we frame these conversations around privilege, as it can be alienating to those who feel like they're being blamed for simply being who they are. Can we find a way to discuss these issues without making people feel like they're being attacked for their "ability privilege" or whatever other kind of privilege?

column7:
>  its proponents want it to simultaneously be a currency and an investment, but these are mutually exclusive goals. A currency is best when it facilitates and encourages exchange, either for consumption or investment in another asset, but an investment is best when it grows, and thus holding it is the superior strategy.

Is a currency that grows in value when held not superior to one that loses value?  

>The better an investment it is, the worse a currency, and vice versa. A currency works when you exchange it, an investment works when you hold it.

I think many people fail to understand what people use a currency for.  That do not primarily use a currency to buy things, they use a currency primarily to store their value until they want to buy things.  You do not "use" money when you buy a cup of coffee, you begin using money when you accept it and continue using it while you hold it.  You stop using it when you exchange it for a cup of coffee and now someone else has begun using it.

> 
> For example: Bitcoin is inherently deflationary because of its fixed supply, and so spending is disincentivized. Any economy based around it will lag compared to one with a currency with a stable or slightly-inflationary one, since economic growth is reliant on commerce.

I agree Bitcoin or any decentralized currency would discourage spending (consumption).  It would also raise the bar for investments--that is with a deflationary currency an investment must offer a higher return than it would otherwise with an inflationary currency.  Where I disagree with you is I see these two things as major positives.  The deflationary economy would have much lower consumption and would only be making quality investments.  People would also be trying to work harder to earn the deflationary currency and the savings rate would be *much* higher.

> Similarly, why would you invest your money in something, like a business or your own education, when you can hide it under your bed, and its value grows on its own? Deflationary currencies thus also disincentivize actual investment, 

No, it disincentivizes low quality investments.  If I have a deflationary currency, I would only be interested in investments that could preform better ^(on a risk a adjusted basis) than the return I would receive from simply holding the currency.  Basically only really good investments would compel people to invest their precious deflationary capital 

> Also, the ultimate demand for a currency is based on the government demanding tax payments denominated in it. 

This is a claim people often make and it makes no sense to me.  I suppose if government collected dollars and then burned them or held them it would cause people to demand dollars, but the US government is not a net holder of dollars.  The government taxes people--pulling in US dollars on one side, and then spends--spewing out dollars on the other side.  If you draw a boundary around the government and the people so you have two groups trading with each other, the government is necessarily a net exporter of dollars.  Conversely, I think there will be a point where people will not accept dollars from the government and the government will then have to shift to trying to pay people in Bitcoin.  That is, government must pay people in the currency they which to hold, currently that is US dollar but that is rapidly changing as Bitcoin continues to gain market share.  Once people do not want dollars, the government will be severely restricted in terms of what it can do.

The problems with Bitcoin generally revolve around a single contradiction: its proponents want it to simultaneously be a currency and an investment, but these are mutually exclusive goals. A currency is best when it facilitates and encourages exchange, either for consumption or investment in another asset, but an investment is best when it grows, and thus holding it is the superior strategy. The better an investment it is, the worse a currency, and vice versa. A currency works when you exchange it, an investment works when you hold it.

For example: Bitcoin is inherently deflationary because of its fixed supply, and so spending is disincentivized. Any economy based around it will lag compared to one with a currency with a stable or slightly-inflationary value, since economic growth is reliant on commerce. 

Similarly, why would you invest your money in something, like a business or your own education, when you can hide it under your bed, and its value grows on its own? Deflationary currencies thus also disincentivize actual investment, and so hamper or even prevent the production of goods and services, ie. economic growth.

Also, the ultimate demand for a currency is based on the government demanding tax payments denominated in it. As long as the US government, for instance, only accepts US dollars for tax payments, Bitcoin's utility will always be dependent on its exchangeability with the dollar. 

edit: The cruel irony here is that Bitcoin's status *as an investment*, and not just pieces of data worth no more or less than any other pieces of data, is based on the speculative belief that it will one day be a viable currency, and people want to get in on the ground floor. It's only survived as it has because of economic illiteracy, with its fans not understanding the currency/investment contradiction. So, amazingly, it's a speculative investment whose perceived value hinges on the belief it's a viable currency. It's like people buying apple seeds on the assumption they'll grow into orange trees. Once that belief doesn't pan out and enough people wise up, its perceived value as an investment will collapse as well. 

Thus, I'll reverse your prediction: Bitcoin's complete demise is inevitable. Sell now and buy shares of an index or mutual fund. If you remember this post in 10 years and want to thank me for pointing the way off a sinking ship, I accept donations via Venmo and Paypal, but please, only US dollars.

And @glossy-cantaloupe, I agree with you that the investment/currency contradiction is a major issue, but I'm not convinced that Bitcoin's demise is inevitable just yet. What about the potential for decentralized currencies to disrupt traditional financial systems?

Can you please point me to and/or quote where I am offended by criticism? I assure you, I'm not a member of Mensa. I'm merely bringing up counterpoints to your view in an effort to change it. I'm beginning to feel as though you made this post without any intention of considering opposing viewpoints. You've failed to address the vast majority of the points I've made. Nevertheless, I'll give it one last go, and I'll be as concise as possible for your convenience:

1. Can you give evidence that scores of 17-18/18 on the Mensa workout do **not** correlate with higher scores on an IQ test?  
2. Can you, upon rereading the full text of what is said at the end of the test, point to the line suggesting that a score of 17-18/18 clearly indicates a high probability of gaining Mensa membership?
3. Further to our conversation regarding "could" vs. "can," is English your native language?

column7:
You suggest this is a scam but nothing you've written suggests any deceit or fraud on the part of Mensa. I took the test as well just now, I didn't see anything before, during, or after the test that was dishonest. Here's the full text of the message at the end of the test:

>A top score! Excellent! You could achieve a high IQ score if you took a Mensa Supervised IQ test.
>
>There are a variety of reasons why people take the IQ test, many are simply curious or want to challenge themselves, others do so for university applications or CVs, as a high IQ can be beneficial, it can make you stand out as a candidate. Many of those that take the test are pleasantly surprised by their IQ score.
>
>If you would like to obtain an accurate IQ score please click to book a - Mensa Supervised IQ test at a test centre near you.
>
>Those that achieve an IQ score in the top 2% will be offered Mensa membership.  Mensa members benefit from an exceptional programme of intellectual and social events, the informative monthly Mensa Magazine, over 100 Special Interest Groups (SIGs) to join and access to the members area of the website.

They're quite upfront about what kind of an IQ it will take to gain Mensa membership. No where do they suggest that based off of just these 18 questions you might be a good candidate for their organization. You posit that you think everyone will score highly on the test and therefore it constitutes a scam since clearly not everyone will score highly on an IQ test, yet you missed a question yourself.

Where is the deceit requisite for this to be a scam?

>I guess being intelligent has no bearing on your moral fibre.

I'm not sure why this should be a surprise to you. I don't even know that intelligence is correlated with morality, let a lone be causally linked to it.

@free-poplin, I have to agree with @tossed-loader on this one. I took the test out of curiosity and didn't feel like I was being misled at any point. The language used is pretty clear about what the test is and what it's for. Maybe we're just reading too much into it?
